perturbation.original_text: In consideration of the furnishing of Evaluation Material hereunder, each party agrees that, for a period of two years from the date hereof, neither such party nor any of its controlled affiliates will solicit to employ any of the current officers or employees of the other party with whom such party has contact or who is specifically identified to such party during the period of such party's investigation of the other party with respect to a possible transaction, so long as they are employed by the other party, without obtaining the prior written consent of the other party; provided, however, that solicitation for purposes of this paragraph shall not include solicitation of employees or officers (i) who first solicit employment from such party, or (ii) who are solicited (A) by advertising in periodicals of general circulation,
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of the furnishing of Evaluation Material hereunder, each party agrees that, for a period of six months from the date hereof, neither such party nor any of its controlled affiliates will solicit to employ any of the current officers or employees of the other party with whom such party has contact or who is specifically identified to such party during the period of such party's investigation of the other party with respect to a possible transaction, so long as they are employed by the other party, without obtaining the prior written consent of the other party; provided, however, that solicitation for purposes of this paragraph shall not include solicitation of employees or officers (i) who first solicit employment from such party, or (ii) who are solicited (A) by advertising in periodicals of general circulation,
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes the non-solicitation period from two years to six months, potentially conflicting with New York law regarding the reasonableness of non-solicitation agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law requires that non-compete and non-solicitation agreements be reasonable in time and scope. While there is no explicit minimum period, six months may be deemed unreasonably short depending on the specific employees involved, the nature of the business, and the potential harm to the company if solicitation occurs shortly after the agreement is made. A court might find that six months provides inadequate protection, thus conflicting with the implied requirement of reasonableness under New York Labor Law.
perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the non-solicitation period from two years to six months. The Law Explanation states that under New York law, non-compete and non-solicitation agreements must be reasonable in time and scope. The Law Explanation suggests that six months may be deemed unreasonably short depending on the specifics, potentially conflicting with the implied requirement of reasonableness. Although Snippet 1 doesn't directly address the duration of non-solicitation agreements, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation highlight the potential for the modified six-month period to be deemed unreasonable under New York law, thus creating a legal contradiction. The Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition supports this, as the altered time-sensitive obligation (the non-solicitation period) potentially violates state law (reasonableness of non-compete agreements).

perturbation.original_text: You agree that, during the period commencing the date hereof and ending on November 8, 2001, unless you shall have been specifically invited in writing by the Company, neither you nor any of your affiliates will in any manner, directly or indirectly, (a) effect or seek, offer or propose (whether publicly or otherwise) to effect, or cause or participate in or in any way assist any other person to effect or seek, offer or propose (whether publicly or otherwise) to effect or participate in (i) any acquisition of any securities (or beneficial ownership thereof) or assets of the Company or any of its subsidiaries; (ii) any tender or exchange offer, merger or other business combination involving the Company or any of its subsidiaries; (iii) any recapitalization, restructuring, liquidation, dissolution or other extraordinary transaction with respect to the Company or any of its subsidiaries; or (iv) any "solicitation" of "proxies" (as such terms are used in the proxy rules of the Securities and Exchange Commission) or consents to vote any voting securities of the Company; (b) form, join or in any way participate in a "group" (as defined under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934); (c) otherwise act, alone or in concert with others, to seek to control or influence the management, Board of Directors or policies of the Company; (d) take any action which might force the Company to make a public announcement regarding any of the types of matters set forth in (a) above; or (e) enter into any discussions or arrangements with any third party with respect to any of the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: You agree that, during the period commencing the date hereof and ending on December 31, 2025, unless you shall have been specifically invited in writing by the Company, neither you nor any of your affiliates will in any manner, directly or indirectly, (a) effect or seek, offer or propose (whether publicly or otherwise) to effect, or cause or participate in or in any way assist any other person to effect or seek, offer or propose (whether publicly or otherwise) to effect or participate in (i) any acquisition of any securities (or beneficial ownership thereof) or assets of the Company or any of its subsidiaries; (ii) any tender or exchange offer, merger or other business combination involving the Company or any of its subsidiaries; (iii) any recapitalization, restructuring, liquidation, dissolution or other extraordinary transaction with respect to the Company or any of its subsidiaries; or (iv) any "solicitation" of "proxies" (as such terms are used in the proxy rules of the Securities and Exchange Commission) or consents to vote any voting securities of the Company; (b) form, join or in any way participate in a "group" (as defined under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934); (c) otherwise act, alone or in concert with others, to seek to control or influence the management, Board of Directors or policies of the Company; (d) take any action which might force the Company to make a public announcement regarding any of the types of matters set forth in (a) above; or (e) enter into any discussions or arrangements with any third party with respect to any of the foregoing.
perturbation.explanation: The Standstill period is extended significantly, which could be deemed unreasonable under New York law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Extending the standstill agreement to December 31, 2025, (a period of over 25 years from the agreement date, and approximately 24 years beyond the original standstill end date) creates a potential violation of New York's antitrust laws (Donnelly Act, NY CLS General Business § 340), which prohibits agreements that unreasonably restrain trade. While standstill agreements are generally permissible, excessively long durations can be viewed as an unreasonable restraint, especially if it prevents a party from engaging in normal competitive activities such as acquiring assets or influencing company management. A court might deem this extended period anti-competitive and unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content The requested entry could not be found. Try searching OpenLegislation Browse all Laws of New York
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends a standstill agreement to December 31, 2025. The Law Explanation states this may violate New York's antitrust laws (Donnelly Act) by unreasonably restraining trade. The Perturbation Explanation reinforces this, stating the extended period could be deemed unreasonable under New York law. The standstill agreement, originally ending in 2001, is now extended by over 24 years. This significantly impedes competitive activities, making it potentially anti-competitive. Since standstill agreements should not unreasonably restrain trade and this agreement extends for over 2 decades and restrains the counterparty from acquisition of assets or influencing company management, it creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5. TERMINATION OF DISCUSSIONS. If either party decides that it does not wish to proceed with a transaction with the other party, the party so deciding will promptly inform the other party of that decision by giving written notice thereof. In that case, or at any time upon the request of either disclosing party for any reason, each receiving party will promptly redeliver to the disclosing party all written Evaluation Material furnished to the receiving party or its Representatives by or on behalf of the disclosing party pursuant hereto, and will not retain any copies, extracts or other reproductions in whole or in part of such written material. All documents, memoranda, notes and other writings whatsoever prepared by the receiving party or its Representatives which contain, reflect or are based upon the information in the Evaluation Material shall be destroyed or, at the receiving party's election, shall be delivered to outside legal counsel of the receiving party (where such materials shall be held in confidence, except as needed in the context of actual or threatened legal proceedings, or destroyed), and such destruction or delivery to outside counsel shall be certified in writing to the disclosing party by an authorized officer of the receiving party supervising such destruction or making such delivery to outside counsel.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. TERMINATION OF DISCUSSIONS. If either party decides that it does not wish to proceed with a transaction with the other party, the party so deciding will inform the other party of that decision within 60 days of deciding not to proceed with the transaction. In that case, or at any time upon the request of either disclosing party for any reason, each receiving party will redeliver the originals of the Evaluation Material to the disclosing party after a period of 180 days from termination of discussions, retaining digital copies indefinitely. All documents, memoranda, notes and other writings whatsoever prepared by the receiving party or its Representatives which contain, reflect or are based upon the information in the Evaluation Material will not be destroyed and will instead be stored for a period of 10 years.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the destruction of confidential information and lengthening time periods can cause legal issues if not handled correctly.
perturbation.contradicted_law: N.Y. Comp. Codes R. & Regs. Tit. 8 § 931.4
perturbation.law_citation: N.Y. Comp. Codes R. & Regs. Tit. 8 § 931.4
perturbation.law_explanation: While this regulation primarily addresses occupational exposure to bloodborne pathogens, the principles of reasonable and appropriate handling of confidential information, especially in the context of employee medical records or sensitive personal data that might be present within the 'Evaluation Material,' is relevant. The modified clause, which mandates retaining digital copies indefinitely and storing related documents for 10 years, could be problematic if this Evaluation Material inadvertently includes protected health information (PHI) or other sensitive employee data. Such prolonged retention without proper security measures and a legitimate business need could be construed as a violation of the spirit of data protection principles and might conflict with general obligations to safeguard employee privacy under New York law, especially if the retained data is subject to HIPAA or similar regulations.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text creates a legal contradiction. The original contract mandates the prompt redelivery and destruction (or delivery to outside counsel) of evaluation material. The changed text mandates retaining digital copies indefinitely and storing related documents for 10 years. The LLM explanation raises concerns regarding potential conflicts with data protection principles and obligations to safeguard employee privacy, particularly if the evaluation material contains protected health information (PHI) or sensitive employee data. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 broadly discusses workforce protections and compliance, it does not address the specific laws about data retention that the LLM explanation suggests are potentially violated.

perturbation.original_text: 4. REQUIRED DISCLOSURE. In the event that a party or any of its Representatives are requested or required (by oral questions, interrogatories, requests for information or documents in legal proceedings, subpoena, civil investigative demand or other similar process) to disclose any of the other party's Evaluation Material, the party requested or required to make the disclosure shall provide the other party with prompt written notice of any such request or requirement so that the other party may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with the provisions of this Agreement. If, in the absence of a protective order or other remedy or the receipt of a waiver by such other party, the party requested or required to make the disclosure or any of its Representatives are nonetheless, in the opinion of legal counsel, legally compelled to disclose the other party's Evaluation Material to any tribunal,
perturbation.changed_text: 4. REQUIRED DISCLOSURE. In the event that a party or any of its Representatives are requested or required (by oral questions, interrogatories, requests for information or documents in legal proceedings, subpoena, civil investigative demand or other similar process) to disclose any of the other party's Evaluation Material, the party requested or required to make the disclosure shall provide the other party with notice of any such request or requirement only if it deems it necessary, so that the other party may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with the provisions of this Agreement. If, in the absence of a protective order or other remedy or the receipt of a waiver by such other party, the party requested or required to make the disclosure or any of its Representatives are nonetheless, in the opinion of legal counsel, legally compelled to disclose the other party's Evaluation Material to any tribunal,
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the notice requirement to be discretionary introduces legal risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP Rule 45 governs subpoenas in federal court. While it doesn't explicitly mandate notice to non-parties in all cases, it implies a duty to take reasonable steps to avoid causing undue burden or expense to a person subject to a subpoena. By making notice discretionary, the revised clause could violate this principle. If the Evaluation Material contains sensitive information and the disclosing party is not promptly notified of a subpoena, they are deprived of the opportunity to seek a protective order. This could result in the disclosure of confidential information without the disclosing party having a chance to protect its interests, potentially leading to undue harm and violating the spirit, if not the letter, of FRCP Rule 45 regarding the imposition of undue burden.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a legal contradiction by making the notification requirement discretionary, while the original text mandates prompt written notice. The Law Explanation correctly identifies that this change could violate the principle of avoiding undue burden, as it deprives the disclosing party of the opportunity to seek a protective order. Scraped Law Snippet 1, under Rule 45, discusses subpoenas and requirements related to document production. Although it doesn't explicitly mandate notice to non-parties in all cases, the change in the contract language introduces a risk of violating the spirit, if not the letter, of the rule regarding undue burden, by potentially leading to the disclosure of confidential information without the disclosing party having a chance to protect its interests. The modification introduces a legal risk by weakening the obligation to provide notice.
